original_abstract: A practical and atom-economical method for the synthesis of benzoxepines is reported, utilizing a rhodium(III)-catalyzed terminal C–H functionalization of easily accessible *ortho*-vinylphenols. Under the optimized conditions—employing [Cp*RhCl2]₂ as the catalyst and Cu(OAc)₂ as the oxidant in acetonitrile—these substrates undergo a formal (5+2) cycloaddition with various alkynes. The reaction demonstrates broad functional group tolerance and high regioselectivity with unsymmetrical alkynes, providing streamlined access to the seven-membered oxepine core found in numerous pharmacologically relevant natural products. Furthermore, the protocol can be adapted for the synthesis of coumarins via a formal (5+1) cycloaddition by substituting the alkyne component with carbon monoxide. Mechanistic studies, including competition experiments and the evaluation of non-conjugated analogues, suggest a pathway involving an initial phenol coordination followed by a unique C–H activation step that likely proceeds through a dearomatization/aromatization sequence.
humanized_abstract: Herein, we develop an efficient and highly atom-economical approach to produce benzoxepines by using a rhodium(III) catalyzed terminal C–H functionalization of readily available *ortho*-vinylphenols. A formal (5+2) cycloaddition of these substrates toward various alkynes proceeds smoothly under optimized rhodium catalysis using the precatalyst [Cp*RhCl2]₂ in combination with Cu(OAc)₂ as oxidant in acetonitrile and gives the expected 7-ring oxepine core containing many bioactive natural products in excellent regioselectivity that endures for a wide range of functional groups. More importantly, using CO as alkyne analogue enables for the formal (5+1) cycloaddition toward forming the core structure of biologically relevant coumarins. We deduced the catalytic mechanism through competitions studies using non-conjugated substrate derivatives along with coordinating sites such as phenol. Our experimental evidence reveals a potential catalytic pathway initiating by the coordination of substrate phenol to RhIII catalyst and involves subsequent unique C–H activation assisted potentially through a dearomatization/aromatization process.

More importantly, using CO as alkyne analogue enables for the formal (5+1) cycloaddition toward forming the core structure of biologically relevant coumarins. We deduced the catalytic mechanism through competitions studies using non-conjugated substrate derivatives along with coordinating sites such as phenol. Our experimental evidence reveals a potential catalytic pathway initiating by the coordination of substrate phenol to RhIII catalyst and involves subsequent unique C–H activation assisted potentially through a dearomatization/aromatization process.
